topicArea: Museums, Provenance & What Kind of Keeping Counts as Care

questions.passage:
The bronze sits in climate-controlled glass, lit like a jewel, and the label beneath it reads: acquired, 1899. The visitor moves on in eight seconds, which studies say is the average, but the little verb stays behind in the case, doing its quiet work on everyone who passes. Acquired is a word that has finished all its arguments in private. It does not say bought, and it does not say taken, and the difference between those two histories is the difference between a museum and a warehouse of evidence. Every museum is both, in proportions it has historically preferred not to calculate.

The museum's founding promise remains a serious one. Objects gathered in public trust are preserved by expertise few source contexts could fund, displayed to anyone who walks in, and studied by scholars who need the world in one building. The universal museum's defenders make the promise philosophical: great objects belong to humanity entire, the encyclopedic collection lets civilizations converse across a corridor, and a child in one hemisphere can stand before the achievements of another without a passport. Dispersal, on this view, is not justice but loss, the conversation dissolved back into monologues.

Provenance is where the promise meets the ledger. Objects arrived by purchase, bequest, excavation, partition agreements of varying honesty, and, in a number no serious institution now disputes, by conquest, coercion, and sales conducted at gunpoint's convenience. Provenance research, once a curatorial afterthought conducted between larger duties, has in the last generation become a rigorous discipline of its own, tracing bills of sale, expedition diaries, and customs stamps, and what it keeps finding is that the polite verb on the label compresses a chain of hands, some of them clean, some of them not, and that the museum's title to many of its treasures is exactly as good as the worst link in the chain.

The case for return begins with what the object is to the people it left. A mask that a gallery presents as sculpture may be, to its source community, a working thing: an ancestor present rather than represented, a participant in ceremonies that halted when it was carried off. The claim is not that the object is beautiful and missed but that it is functional and absent, patrimony in the living sense, and that continued retention, however careful, continues the original taking by other means. Recent decades have seen returns negotiated on precisely these grounds, and the sky over the lending institutions has conspicuously failed to fall.

The universalist rebuttal has real content and real problems. The floodgates worry, return one thing and empty the halls, treats every claim as identical, which the claims are not. The preservation argument, that some objects are safer where they are, is sometimes true and always awkward, since the guardian pleading safety is in several famous instances the reason the object needed guarding. The genuinely hard questions are quieter: who counts as heir when the kingdom that lost the object no longer exists, and what a vitrine's explanatory card can honestly substitute for the ceremony an object was made to inhabit, context restored in prose being a different thing from context restored in fact.

Between keep-everything and return-everything a working middle has been assembling itself: long-term loans that concede title while sharing presence, joint custody rotating an object between continents, replicas and high-resolution surrogates standing in at whichever end accepts standing-in, and the slow rethinking of deaccessioning, long the profession's deepest taboo, as sometimes the most honest entry in the catalogue. The vocabulary is shifting from owner to steward, and stewardship, unlike ownership, is a role that can be audited: it has duties, and the duties can be failed.

Back in the gallery, the label has been rewritten at some institutions, and the new cards say more: how the object came, through whose hands, under what shadow. The candor costs something and buys more, because a museum's authority was never really its walls or its title deeds; it was the public's belief that the keeping is care. The question pressing on the profession is not whether museums should exist, since the best of what they do remains irreplaceable, but what kind of keeping counts as care, and whose consent the caring now requires.
questions.stem: Which of the following best expresses the main idea of the passage?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Because so many prized museum objects arrived through conquest and open coercion, today's encyclopedic collections should be dispersed entirely back to their source communities.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Provenance research has become a scholarly discipline capable of tracing an object's chain of custody through bills of sale and expedition records.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The museum's promise and the restitution case both have force; the emerging resolution is stewardship over ownership, the question being what keeping counts as care.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The universal museum's defenders have decisively answered restitution claims by showing that dispersal would dissolve the conversation between civilizations.

explanations.A: This choice converts the passage's provenance findings into the return-everything pole it declines to occupy. The tainted chains are documented, but the founding promise is called serious, the best of what museums do irreplaceable, and the working middle, loans, joint custody, stewardship, is the destination, not dispersal.
explanations.B: This choice promotes one paragraph's discipline into the thesis. Provenance research supplies the ledger where promise meets history, but the passage's argument runs onward through the restitution case, the rebuttal's problems, and the stewardship turn; the tracing is instrument, not conclusion.
explanations.C: Correct. The passage credits the universal museum's promise, weighs it against the ledger of arrival and the living-patrimony case for return, finds the rebuttal partly sound and partly awkward, and lands on the assembling middle: steward rather than owner, a role with auditable duties, with the closing question not whether museums should exist but what kind of keeping counts as care and whose consent it requires.
explanations.D: This choice awards the universalists a victory the fifth paragraph withholds. Their rebuttal has real content and real problems, the floodgates worry overgeneralized, the safety plea awkward from guardians who caused the danger; nothing in the passage calls the answer decisive.

questions.stem: The primary function of the fifth paragraph is to:

choices.label: A
choices.text: assess the universalist rebuttal, granting its real content while exposing its problems and relocating the hard questions.

choices.label: B
choices.text: demonstrate that the persistent floodgates worry has successfully prevented all recent restitution negotiations from concluding.

choices.label: C
choices.text: argue that preservation expertise settles the question of where contested objects belong.

choices.label: D
choices.text: reject the restitution case presented in the fourth paragraph as sentimental.

explanations.A: Correct. The paragraph announces its double verdict, real content and real problems, then delivers both: the floodgates worry treats unlike claims as identical, the safety plea is sometimes true and always awkward given the guardian's history, and the genuinely hard questions are quieter, who counts as heir when the losing kingdom is gone, and whether prose on a card can substitute for the ceremony the object was made to inhabit.
explanations.B: This choice contradicts the fourth paragraph's record. Returns have been negotiated in recent decades and the sky conspicuously failed to fall; the floodgates worry is assessed as an overgeneralization, not credited with stopping anything.
explanations.C: This choice hardens the paragraph's most qualified concession into a verdict. The preservation argument is sometimes true and always awkward, undercut by the guardian who caused the danger; nothing in the paragraph lets expertise settle location.
explanations.D: This choice invents a dismissal the paragraph never makes. The restitution case arrives grounded in function, not sentiment, an ancestor present rather than represented, and the fifth paragraph engages its opponents' rebuttal rather than reversing the fourth's account.

questions.stem: The observation that acquired is a word that has finished all its arguments in private, in the first paragraph, most nearly conveys that:

choices.label: A
choices.text: museum labels are drafted by committees whose deliberations are not published.

choices.label: B
choices.text: visitors spend too little time reading labels to question their wording.

choices.label: C
choices.text: acquisition disputes between museums and claimants are typically settled through confidential legal agreements.

choices.label: D
choices.text: the label's neutral verb conceals a contested history, presenting a chain of hands as settled.

explanations.A: This choice literalizes the figure into institutional procedure. The privacy in question is the verb's, not a committee's; the sentence concerns what the word suppresses, the difference between bought and taken, not how label copy gets approved.
explanations.B: This choice borrows the eight-second statistic for the wrong job. The visitor's brevity is why the verb's work goes unnoticed, but the finished arguments are the histories the word declines to name, not a reading-time problem.
explanations.C: This choice converts a linguistic observation into a legal one. No settlements or agreements appear; the paragraph's subject is euphemism, the polite verb that the third paragraph shows compressing a chain of hands, some clean, some not.
explanations.D: Correct. The sentence unpacks itself in the lines around it: acquired does not say bought and does not say taken, and that difference separates a museum from a warehouse of evidence, in proportions institutions have preferred not to calculate. The verb presents as closed a question the passage spends its length reopening, which is why rewritten labels in the final paragraph say how the object came and under what shadow.

questions.stem: According to the second paragraph, defenders of the universal museum hold that:

choices.label: A
choices.text: source communities generally lack the specialized professional preservation expertise required to receive returned objects safely and to house them well.

choices.label: B
choices.text: great objects belong to humanity entire, the encyclopedic collection lets civilizations converse, and dispersal would be loss.

choices.label: C
choices.text: a museum's title to its holdings is only ever as good as the worst link in an object's long chain of custody.

choices.label: D
choices.text: objects function as living patrimony whose absence halts the ceremonies they served.

explanations.A: This choice hands the second paragraph the fifth's most awkward argument. The safety plea appears later, flagged as sometimes true and always awkward; the founding promise as stated is philosophical and generous, not a judgment about recipients' competence.
explanations.B: Correct. The paragraph attributes the position in its own three movements: great objects belong to humanity entire, the encyclopedic collection lets civilizations converse across a corridor, a child standing before another hemisphere's achievements without a passport, and dispersal, on this view, is not justice but loss, the conversation dissolved back into monologues.
explanations.C: This choice assigns the universalists their opponents' ledger. The worst-link principle is the third paragraph's provenance finding, the discovery that unsettles the promise; defenders of the encyclopedic collection are not quoted conceding it.
explanations.D: This choice attributes the restitution case to the party it presses against. Living patrimony, the ancestor present rather than represented, is the fourth paragraph's argument for return, precisely what the universalist position must answer.

questions.stem: A museum facing a restitution claim proposes rotating the contested object between its own galleries and the claimant community on a fixed schedule, with title formally transferred to the community. Based on the sixth paragraph, the author would most likely characterize this proposal as:

choices.label: A
choices.text: a floodgates risk, since other communities will demand identical arrangements.

choices.label: B
choices.text: an instance of the assembling middle, conceding title while sharing presence, stewardship over ownership.

choices.label: C
choices.text: a violation of the deaccessioning taboo that the profession still enforces.

choices.label: D
choices.text: an evasion of the underlying claim, since only unconditional physical return can answer a living-patrimony argument.

explanations.A: This choice deploys the worry the fifth paragraph disarms. Treating every claim as identical is named as the floodgates argument's flaw; the sixth paragraph presents negotiated arrangements as the working middle, not as precedents to be feared.
explanations.B: Correct. The proposal matches the sixth paragraph's inventory almost term for term: joint custody rotating an object between continents, arrangements that concede title while sharing presence, the vocabulary shifting from owner to steward. Title transferred, presence shared on schedule, is stewardship in the auditable sense the paragraph describes, a role with duties rather than deeds.
explanations.C: This choice cites the taboo the paragraph shows dissolving. Deaccessioning is being rethought as sometimes the most honest entry in the catalogue; invoking the old prohibition against an arrangement the paragraph would house among its middle paths reverses the direction of the argument.
explanations.D: This choice imposes an all-or-nothing standard the passage never adopts. The fourth paragraph grounds the claim; the sixth answers with negotiated forms, loans, custody, surrogates, offered as resolutions rather than evasions, and the author endorses the middle's assembly.

questions.stem: Which of the following, if true, would most weaken the second paragraph's claim that the encyclopedic collection lets civilizations converse?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Visitor studies finding objects viewed in isolation, cross-cultural comparison almost never occurring, comprehension unimproved by encyclopedic display.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Evidence that major encyclopedic museums charge substantial admission fees that measurably limit public attendance.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Evidence that most large encyclopedic museums display only a small fraction of their total collected holdings to the visiting public at any given moment.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Surveys finding that curators disagree about how galleries should be arranged.

explanations.A: Correct. The conversation claim is a claim about what proximity produces: civilizations conversing across a corridor, understanding generated by assembly. Studies finding isolation the normal viewing mode, comparison almost never occurring, and comprehension unimproved would show the corridor carrying no conversation, the promise's mechanism failing at the only point where it could operate, in the visitor.
explanations.B: This choice narrows the audience without touching the mechanism. Fees limit who enters; the claim concerns what happens for those who do, and a conversation that occurs behind a paid door is still a conversation.
explanations.C: This choice measures storage against display, which every large collection concedes; the conversing is claimed for what hangs, not for the reserves, and a fraction shown can still assemble hemispheres in one building.
explanations.D: This choice documents professional disagreement about arrangement, which presupposes the galleries' purpose rather than testing it; curators disputing layouts is the ordinary business of institutions whose displays are assumed to matter.

questions.stem: Which of the following findings would most strengthen the fourth paragraph's claim that contested objects function as living patrimony rather than as art in absence?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Evidence that returned objects appreciate in market value after repatriation.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Surveys finding that source-community members regularly visit the holding museums when traveling internationally.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Evidence that claimant communities negotiating for objects also frequently request copies of the original expedition photographs and field notes.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Ethnographies documenting ceremonies suspended during an object's absence, resuming on return, the community addressing the item as a present ancestor.

explanations.A: This choice prices the object, which is the gallery's frame rather than the community's. Market appreciation treats the item as art with a provenance story; the claim is precisely that the object's value is functional and relational, not auctionable.
explanations.B: This choice shows attachment surviving displacement, which both sides could cite; visiting an object in a vitrine is compatible with mourning a working thing or admiring a sculpture, and distinguishes neither.
explanations.C: This choice adds an archival request beside the object claim, which says nothing about the object's living function; photographs document, and documentation is the museum's own register.
explanations.D: Correct. The claim's content is functional: an ancestor present rather than represented, a participant in ceremonies that halted when it was carried off. Ceremonies suspended for exactly the absence, resuming upon return, with the community addressing rather than exhibiting the item, is that functionality observed end to end, absence producing the halt and presence the resumption, the working thing caught working.
